planting.note: Direct sow in fall onto dry, rocky, well-drained soil; winter cold provides natural stratification and supports reliable spring germination.

directions: Direct sow in fall onto dry, rocky or sandy, slightly alkaline to neutral, very well-drained soil in full sun, allowing winter temperatures to cold-stratify seed naturally before spring germination. Once established, thin plants to 18-24 inches and provide no supplemental irrigation, as this species is adapted to the parched mountain slopes and canyon edges of New Mexico, Arizona, and adjacent Mexico where it is native. Dense, upright spikes of pale pink to lavender-pink, anise-scented tubular flowers appear from summer through fall and draw hummingbirds, monarch butterflies, and native bumblebees in great numbers. The broad, aromatic leaves carry a pleasant anise-mint flavor and can be dried and brewed as herbal tea; this species is far more drought-tolerant than Agastache foeniculum and thrives where other hyssops fail.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center Native Plant Database
Flora of North America Vol. 21 — Agastache pallidiflora

planting.note: Transplant rooted cuttings in spring into dry, lean, sandy, slightly acidic, very well-drained soil; avoid clay and enriched soils.

directions: Propagate from stem cuttings taken in late spring or summer rather than from seed, as germination is erratic and slow; root cuttings in clean sand and transplant rooted starts in spring into dry, lean, sandy, slightly acidic, very well-drained soil in full sun. Avoid clay, enriched, or moisture-retentive soils, as the species is endemic to sandy and gravelly river bars along the Big South Fork and upper Cumberland River system in Tennessee and Kentucky, where it is federally listed as threatened. Dense mats of wiry, upright stems bear tiny, needle-like, intensely rosemary-scented leaves and masses of small, pale pink to lavender, 2-lipped mint flowers in spring; the entire plant releases a strong fragrance when brushed. An outstanding native aromatic subshrub for rock gardens and sandy borders in zones 5-8 where its narrow habitat requirements of sharp drainage, lean soil, and full sun can be met.
sources: U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service — Conradina verticillata Species Profile
Flora of North America Vol. 21 — Conradina

planting.note: Direct sow in fall onto thin, rocky or sandy, calcareous to slightly acidic, very well-drained soil; winter cold provides natural stratification.

directions: Direct sow in fall onto thin, rocky or sandy, calcareous to slightly acidic, very well-drained soil in full sun, pressing seed lightly onto the surface and allowing winter cold to stratify it naturally before spring germination. This diminutive native perennial is one of the smallest-statured Scutellaria species in eastern North America, at home in cedar glades, thin-soiled limestone outcrops, and calcareous prairies from the eastern seaboard through the central states. Tiny, tubular, blue to violet-blue, 2-lipped flowers appear on thread-like stems with small, rounded, often toothed leaves from May through July. Plants spread slowly by shallow rhizomes to form loose, low colonies and are excellent in rock garden crevices, glade restorations, and dry, infertile native plant settings where taller competitors are kept in check.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center Native Plant Database
Flora of North America Vol. 21 — Scutellaria parvula

planting.note: Direct sow in fall onto dry, lean, rocky or sandy, well-drained soil in full sun; seeds overwinter and germinate in early spring.

directions: Direct sow in fall onto dry, lean, rocky or sandy, calcareous to neutral, well-drained soil in full sun; seeds overwinter in place and germinate in early spring as one of the earliest-blooming native wildflowers in the Texas Hill Country and adjacent south-central states. Wiry, branching, low-growing stems with small, ovate leaves bear axillary pairs of tiny, bright blue to violet-blue, 2-lipped flowers from early spring through May, appearing before most other wildflowers in open, disturbed, and roadside ground. Avoid fertile or moisture-retentive soils, which produce rank, floppy growth and reduce flowering; this species thrives in the leanest, sunniest, and most exposed conditions available. Allow seed heads to shatter in place, as the plant self-sows freely in open, bare, or gravelly ground and naturalizes readily in rock gardens and dry native garden borders.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center Native Plant Database
Diggs, Lipscomb & O'Kennon — Illustrated Flora of North Central Texas

planting.note: Direct sow in fall into moist, humus-rich, shaded woodland soil; winter cold stratifies seed naturally for spring germination.

directions: Direct sow in fall into moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral woodland soil in partial to full shade, allowing winter cold to stratify seed naturally before spring germination; keep soil consistently moist through the first season until plants are well established. This native mint-family perennial is at home on shaded slopes, stream banks, and rock ledges throughout the Appalachians and eastern mountain states, where its square stems with opposite, softly hairy, toothed leaves are diagnostic. Whorled clusters of small, pink to rose-pink, 2-lipped flowers appear along the upper stems in summer and are of particular value to long-tongued bumblebees and bumblebee specialists that cannot access shallower flowers. An understated but ecologically important native shade mint for woodland gardens and naturalized stream bank plantings in zones 4-8.
sources: Flora of North America Vol. 21 — Stachys nuttallii
